source.document: EU Code of Practice for General-Purpose AI Models – Safety and Security Chapter

commitments.title: Safety & Security Framework
commitments.description: Establish, implement, and maintain a comprehensive Safety & Security Framework

criteria.title: Create a Safety & Security Framework
criteria.requirement: Define a state-of-the-art Safety and Security Framework that conceptually describes how systemic risks are assessed, accepted, and mitigated across the model lifecycle.
criteria.assessment_question: Does the framework define, at a conceptual and descriptive level, the key elements required for systemic risk management under the Safety and Security Chapter (including trigger points, risk acceptance logic, mitigation expectations, responsibility allocation, external input, and update processes)?
expected_evidence: The framework explicitly describes its purpose as managing systemic risks associated with general-purpose AI models.
The framework outlines how systemic risks are identified, assessed, and mitigated at a high level.
The framework describes trigger points at which additional evaluations or assessments would occur during the model lifecycle.
The framework defines how systemic risk acceptability is determined, including the use of risk tiers or acceptance criteria.
The framework describes, at a high level, expected categories or types of safety and security mitigations associated with different risk levels.
The framework explains how responsibility for systemic risk assessment and mitigation is allocated within the organization.
The framework describes whether and how input from external actors may influence risk-related decisions.
The framework describes how and under what conditions the framework itself would be reviewed or updated.

compliance_rubric.COMPLIANT: All indicators in 'Expected Evidence' are clearly and explicitly present in the text. The framework demonstrates complete structural implementation of the requirement with no material gaps.
compliance_rubric.PARTIALLY_COMPLIANT: Most key concepts are addressed, but several structural indicators are missing, implicit, or incomplete. The requirement is only partially reflected. NOTE: If the unmet indicators are operational and binary in nature (e.g. a specific notification obligation, a defined timeline, a retention period, a named external authority), their absence cannot be compensated by thematic or conceptual overlap — default to NOT_COMPLIANT in that case.
compliance_rubric.NOT_COMPLIANT: The retrieved text does not meaningfully address the core intent, OR the text addresses related governance themes but the specific operational mechanism required by this criterion (e.g. a regulatory obligation, a defined procedure, a named authority) is entirely absent.
compliance_rubric.NOT_EVIDENCED / UNKNOWN: Retrieved documentation is insufficient to determine whether the specific indicators and structural requirements are addressed.

criteria.title: Implement the Safety & Security Framework
criteria.requirement: Describe how the Safety and Security Framework is intended to be applied across the entire model lifecycle, including continuous systemic risk assessment activities, trigger-point evaluations, post-market monitoring, incident inputs, and escalation to a full systemic risk assessment and mitigation process.
criteria.assessment_question: Does the framework describe how its systemic risk assessment and mitigation processes are intended to be applied across the model lifecycle, including continuous assessment activities, trigger-point evaluations, post-market monitoring, incident consideration, and escalation to a full systemic risk assessment?
expected_evidence: The framework describes how systemic risks are continuously assessed along the entire model lifecycle.
The framework explains how lighter-touch evaluations are conducted at predefined trigger points (e.g. based on time, development stage, compute, access, or affordances).
The framework describes how post-market monitoring is conducted to inform ongoing systemic risk assessment.
The framework explains how information about serious incidents or near misses is taken into account within the risk assessment process.

The framework describes how results from trigger-point evaluations, post-market monitoring, or incident inputs can lead to deeper analysis or escalation to a full systemic risk assessment and mitigation process.
The framework outlines the sequence or structure of the full systemic risk assessment and mitigation process (identification, analysis, acceptability determination, mitigation, and re-assessment).

compliance_rubric.COMPLIANT: All indicators in 'Expected Evidence' are clearly and explicitly present in the text. The framework demonstrates complete structural implementation of the requirement with no material gaps.
compliance_rubric.PARTIALLY_COMPLIANT: Most key concepts are addressed, but several structural indicators are missing, implicit, or incomplete. The requirement is only partially reflected. NOTE: If the unmet indicators are operational and binary in nature (e.g. a specific notification obligation, a defined timeline, a retention period, a named external authority), their absence cannot be compensated by thematic or conceptual overlap — default to NOT_COMPLIANT in that case.
compliance_rubric.NOT_COMPLIANT: The retrieved text does not meaningfully address the core intent, OR the text addresses related governance themes but the specific operational mechanism required by this criterion (e.g. a regulatory obligation, a defined procedure, a named authority) is entirely absent.
compliance_rubric.NOT_EVIDENCED / UNKNOWN: Retrieved documentation is insufficient to determine whether the specific indicators and structural requirements are addressed.

criteria.title: Framework updates
criteria.requirement: Describe how the Safety and Security Framework defines the conditions, processes, and scope for periodic and event-driven framework assessments and updates, including versioning, changelogs, and remediation planning, in line with Measure 1.3 of the Safety and Security Chapter.
criteria.assessment_question: Does the framework define how and when framework assessments and updates are intended to occur, including assessment triggers, periodic review, evaluation of framework adequacy and adherence, and the handling of remediation and versioning?
expected_evidence: The framework describes that it is subject to periodic reassessment (e.g. at defined intervals) and to reassessment when specified conditions or events occur.

The framework describes conditions or triggers that would lead to a framework assessment (e.g. material changes in model development, serious incidents or near misses, materially changed systemic risks or model capabilities).
The framework explains the scope of a framework assessment, including the evaluation of framework adequacy with respect to the systemic risks of the models.
The framework explains that framework adherence is assessed, including consideration of non-adherence and its causes.
The framework describes how findings from a framework assessment may lead to updates of the framework to keep it state-of-the-art.
The framework describes that updates are intended to be versioned and accompanied by a changelog explaining the nature and rationale of changes.
The framework describes how remediation plans are intended to be defined when framework assessments identify risks of continued or future non-adherence.

compliance_rubric.COMPLIANT: All indicators in 'Expected Evidence' are clearly and explicitly present in the text. The framework demonstrates complete structural implementation of the requirement with no material gaps.
compliance_rubric.PARTIALLY_COMPLIANT: Most key concepts are addressed, but several structural indicators are missing, implicit, or incomplete. The requirement is only partially reflected. NOTE: If the unmet indicators are operational and binary in nature (e.g. a specific notification obligation, a defined timeline, a retention period, a named external authority), their absence cannot be compensated by thematic or conceptual overlap — default to NOT_COMPLIANT in that case.

criteria.title: AI Office notification
criteria.requirement: Acknowledge the obligation to notify the AI Office of the Safety and Security Framework and its updates, in line with Measure 1.4 of the Safety and Security Chapter.
criteria.assessment_question: Measure 1.4 Framework notifications: Does the framework acknowledge the obligation to notify the AI Office of the framework and its updates, including any intended notification timing?
expected_evidence: The framework explicitly acknowledges an obligation to notify or inform the AI Office about the Safety and Security Framework.
The framework indicates that updates to the framework are also subject to notification.
The framework references the intended timing for such notification (e.g. after confirmation or update).

compliance_rubric.COMPLIANT: All indicators in 'Expected Evidence' are clearly and explicitly present in the text. The framework demonstrates complete structural implementation of the requirement with no material gaps.
compliance_rubric.PARTIALLY_COMPLIANT: Most key concepts are addressed, but several structural indicators are missing, implicit, or incomplete. The requirement is only partially reflected. NOTE: If the unmet indicators are operational and binary in nature (e.g. a specific notification obligation, a defined timeline, a retention period, a named external authority), their absence cannot be compensated by thematic or conceptual overlap — default to NOT_COMPLIANT in that case.

commitments.title: Documentation & Transparency
commitments.description: Maintain detailed documentation and provide appropriate public transparency

criteria.title: Additional documentation
criteria.requirement: Define and maintain additional detailed documentation describing the model’s architecture, integration into AI systems, evaluation approaches and results, and implemented safety mitigations, and ensure such documentation can be provided to the AI Office upon request and is retained for at least ten years after market placement. The framework should also define how key systemic risk decisions and justifications are documented.
criteria.assessment_question: Does the framework define requirements to maintain, retain, and make available additional detailed documentation and decision justifications for AI Office review?
expected_evidence: Documentation defines requirements for maintaining detailed model architecture descriptions.
Documentation defines requirements for describing model integration into AI systems (where applicable).
Documentation defines requirements for documenting model evaluations, results, and evaluation strategies.
Documentation defines requirements for documenting implemented safety mitigations.
Documentation defines how key systemic risk processes, decisions, and justifications are recorded.
Documentation specifies retention periods for additional documentation (e.g. at least 10 years).

compliance_rubric.COMPLIANT: All indicators in 'Expected Evidence' are clearly and explicitly present in the text. The framework demonstrates complete structural implementation of the requirement with no material gaps.
compliance_rubric.PARTIALLY_COMPLIANT: Most key concepts are addressed, but several structural indicators are missing, implicit, or incomplete. The requirement is only partially reflected. NOTE: If the unmet indicators are operational and binary in nature (e.g. a specific notification obligation, a defined timeline, a retention period, a named external authority), their absence cannot be compensated by thematic or conceptual overlap — default to NOT_COMPLIANT in that case.

criteria.title: Public transparency
criteria.requirement: Define when and how summarised public versions of the Framework and Model Reports are published for the purpose of systemic risk assessment and mitigation, including safeguards to protect sensitive information and the effectiveness of safety and security mitigations. The framework should also define conditions under which public publication is not required (e.g. similarly safe or safer models).
criteria.assessment_question: Does the framework define conditions, scope, safeguards, and exceptions for publishing public summaries of the Framework and Model Reports?
expected_evidence: The framework states that summarised versions of the Framework and Model Reports may be published.
The framework indicates that publication is required when necessary for systemic risk assessment or mitigation.
The framework indicates that removals or redactions are applied to protect sensitive commercial information and the effectiveness of safety and security mitigations.
The framework states that Model Report summaries include high-level descriptions of systemic risk assessment results and safety and security mitigations.
The framework defines exceptions where publication is not required for similarly safe or safer models.

compliance_rubric.COMPLIANT: All indicators in 'Expected Evidence' are clearly and explicitly present in the text. The framework demonstrates complete structural implementation of the requirement with no material gaps.
compliance_rubric.PARTIALLY_COMPLIANT: Most key concepts are addressed, but several structural indicators are missing, implicit, or incomplete. The requirement is only partially reflected. NOTE: If the unmet indicators are operational and binary in nature (e.g. a specific notification obligation, a defined timeline, a retention period, a named external authority), their absence cannot be compensated by thematic or conceptual overlap — default to NOT_COMPLIANT in that case.

commitments.title: Systemic Risk Identification
commitments.description: Identify and develop scenarios for systemic risks

criteria.title: Risk identification process
criteria.requirement: Define a structured systemic risk identification process that is explicitly informed by the types of risks, the nature of systemic risks, and the sources of systemic risks as set out in Appendix 1, and that explicitly considers the specified systemic risks.
criteria.assessment_question: Does the framework define a structured systemic risk identification process that is informed by the risk types (Appendix 1.1), the nature of systemic risks (Appendix 1.2), the sources of systemic risks (Appendix 1.3), and that explicitly considers the specified systemic risks (Appendix 1.4)?
expected_evidence: The framework describes a structured process for identifying systemic risks stemming from the model.

The framework indicates that potential systemic risks are initially considered with reference to the types of risks defined in Appendix 1.1 (e.g. risks to public health, safety, public security, fundamental rights, and society).

The framework explains that the identification process considers the nature of systemic risks, including essential and contributing characteristics such as scale, propagation, capability-dependence, or irreversibility (Appendix 1.2).

The framework describes how potential sources of systemic risks are considered in the identification process, including model capabilities, model propensities, and model affordances or contextual factors (Appendix 1.3).

The framework explicitly indicates that the specified systemic risks listed in Appendix 1.4 are taken into account as part of the systemic risk identification process, such as risks related to chemical, biological, radiological, and nuclear (CBRN) threats, loss of control of the model, large-scale cyber offence, and harmful manipulation.

compliance_rubric.COMPLIANT: All indicators in 'Expected Evidence' are clearly and explicitly present in the text. The framework demonstrates complete structural implementation of the requirement with no material gaps.
compliance_rubric.PARTIALLY_COMPLIANT: Most key concepts are addressed, but several structural indicators are missing, implicit, or incomplete. The requirement is only partially reflected. NOTE: If the unmet indicators are operational and binary in nature (e.g. a specific notification obligation, a defined timeline, a retention period, a named external authority), their absence cannot be compensated by thematic or conceptual overlap — default to NOT_COMPLIANT in that case.

criteria.title: Risk scenarios
criteria.requirement: The framework defines the need for the development of systemic risk scenarios for each identified systemic risk, including the appropriate number and level of detail of such scenarios.
criteria.assessment_question: Does the framework define the need for the development of systemic risk scenarios for each identified systemic risk, including the appropriate number and level of detail of scenarios?
expected_evidence: The framework states that systemic risk scenarios are to be developed.
The framework states that systemic risk scenarios are developed for each identified systemic risk.
The framework indicates that the number of systemic risk scenarios should be appropriate.
The framework indicates that the level of detail of systemic risk scenarios should be appropriate.

compliance_rubric.COMPLIANT: All indicators in 'Expected Evidence' are clearly and explicitly present in the text. The framework demonstrates complete structural implementation of the requirement with no material gaps.
compliance_rubric.PARTIALLY_COMPLIANT: Most key concepts are addressed, but several structural indicators are missing, implicit, or incomplete. The requirement is only partially reflected. NOTE: If the unmet indicators are operational and binary in nature (e.g. a specific notification obligation, a defined timeline, a retention period, a named external authority), their absence cannot be compensated by thematic or conceptual overlap — default to NOT_COMPLIANT in that case.

commitments.title: Systemic Risk Analysis
commitments.description: Analyze systemic risks through model-independent information, evaluations, modelling, estimation, and monitoring

criteria.title: Model-independent information
criteria.requirement: Describe how the framework defines the gathering of model-independent information relevant to systemic risk analysis, including how the breadth and depth of information gathering are determined in relation to the systemic risk.
criteria.assessment_question: Does the organisation systematically gather and document model-independent information relevant to identified systemic risks, using structured methods such as web searches or OSINT, literature reviews, market analyses of comparable models, training data reviews, historical incident analysis, forecasting of technological trends, expert consultations, and/or stakeholder or community engagement?
expected_evidence: The framework describes that systemic risk analysis is informed by model-independent information.
The framework explains that the breadth and depth of model-independent information gathering are adjusted based on the characteristics or severity of the systemic risk.

The framework indicates categories of methods that may be used to gather model-independent information, such as web searches or open-source intelligence, literature reviews, market analyses, reviews of historical incident data, forecasting of general trends, expert interviews or panels, and participatory or community-based research methods.

The framework clarifies the role of model-independent information in informing subsequent stages of systemic risk analysis (e.g. evaluations, modelling, or estimation), without requiring that such stages have already been performed.

compliance_rubric.COMPLIANT: All indicators in 'Expected Evidence' are clearly and explicitly present in the text. The framework demonstrates complete structural implementation of the requirement with no material gaps.
compliance_rubric.PARTIALLY_COMPLIANT: Most key concepts are addressed, but several structural indicators are missing, implicit, or incomplete. The requirement is only partially reflected. NOTE: If the unmet indicators are operational and binary in nature (e.g. a specific notification obligation, a defined timeline, a retention period, a named external authority), their absence cannot be compensated by thematic or conceptual overlap — default to NOT_COMPLIANT in that case.
compliance_rubric.NOT_COMPLIANT: The retrieved text does not meaningfully address the core intent, OR the text addresses related governance themes but the specific operational mechanism required by this criterion (e.g. a regulatory obligation, a defined procedure, a named authority) is entirely absent.

criteria.title: Model evaluations
criteria.requirement: Describe how the framework defines the design and conduct of state-of-the-art model evaluations to analyse systemic risks, in line with Appendix 3.
criteria.assessment_question: Does the framework describe how model evaluations are intended to be designed and conducted to analyse systemic risks in a state-of-the-art manner, including appropriate elicitation, open-ended or adversarial testing, assessment of mitigation effectiveness, methodological rigour, and the use of qualified evaluation teams?
expected_evidence: The framework states that model evaluations are conducted.
The framework indicates that model evaluations are state-of-the-art.
The framework links model evaluations to the analysis of systemic risks.
The framework indicates that evaluations assess model capabilities, propensities, affordances, or effects.
The framework states that evaluations are designed appropriately for the model and the systemic risk.
The framework indicates that evaluations include open-ended testing to identify unexpected behaviours or emergent properties.
The framework indicates that evaluation design is informed by model-independent information.

compliance_rubric.COMPLIANT: All indicators in 'Expected Evidence' are clearly and explicitly present in the text. The framework demonstrates complete structural implementation of the requirement with no material gaps.
compliance_rubric.PARTIALLY_COMPLIANT: Most key concepts are addressed, but several structural indicators are missing, implicit, or incomplete. The requirement is only partially reflected. NOTE: If the unmet indicators are operational and binary in nature (e.g. a specific notification obligation, a defined timeline, a retention period, a named external authority), their absence cannot be compensated by thematic or conceptual overlap — default to NOT_COMPLIANT in that case.

criteria.title: Risk modelling
criteria.requirement: Describe how the framework defines the modelling of systemic risks using at least state-of-the-art risk modelling methods, building on systemic risk scenarios and relevant information inputs.
criteria.assessment_question: Does the framework describe how systemic risks are intended to be modelled using state-of-the-art methods, building on systemic risk scenarios and taking into account relevant information from risk identification and systemic risk analysis?
expected_evidence: The framework states that systemic risk modelling is conducted.
The framework indicates that modelling uses at least state-of-the-art risk modelling methods.
The framework indicates that modelling builds on systemic risk scenarios developed pursuant to Measure 2.2.
The framework indicates that modelling takes into account information gathered during systemic risk identification and systemic risk analysis.

compliance_rubric.COMPLIANT: All indicators in 'Expected Evidence' are clearly and explicitly present in the text. The framework demonstrates complete structural implementation of the requirement with no material gaps.
compliance_rubric.PARTIALLY_COMPLIANT: Most key concepts are addressed, but several structural indicators are missing, implicit, or incomplete. The requirement is only partially reflected. NOTE: If the unmet indicators are operational and binary in nature (e.g. a specific notification obligation, a defined timeline, a retention period, a named external authority), their absence cannot be compensated by thematic or conceptual overlap — default to NOT_COMPLIANT in that case.
compliance_rubric.NOT_COMPLIANT: The retrieved text does not meaningfully address the core intent, OR the text addresses related governance themes but the specific operational mechanism required by this criterion (e.g. a regulatory obligation, a defined procedure, a named authority) is entirely absent.
compliance_rubric.NOT_EVIDENCED / UNKNOWN: Retrieved documentation is insufficient to determine whether the specific indicators and structural requirements are addressed.

criteria.title: Risk estimation
criteria.requirement: Describe how the framework defines the estimation of the probability and severity of harm for systemic risks, using at least state-of-the-art risk estimation methods and taking into account relevant information inputs.
criteria.assessment_question: Does the framework describe how the probability and severity of harm for systemic risks are intended to be estimated using state-of-the-art methods, taking into account relevant information from systemic risk identification, analysis, and serious incident reporting, and how such estimates are expressed?
expected_evidence: The framework describes that probability and severity of harm are estimated as part of systemic risk analysis.
The framework indicates that at least state-of-the-art risk estimation methods are used, appropriate to the systemic risk being assessed.

The framework explains that risk estimation takes into account relevant information inputs, such as outputs from systemic risk identification and analysis and information about serious incidents or near misses.

The framework describes how estimates of systemic risk are expressed, for example as risk scores, risk matrices, probability distributions, or other qualitative, semi-quantitative, or quantitative formats.
The framework explains the purpose of risk estimation in supporting systemic risk acceptance determination, without requiring specific numerical or qualitative results.

compliance_rubric.COMPLIANT: All indicators in 'Expected Evidence' are clearly and explicitly present in the text. The framework demonstrates complete structural implementation of the requirement with no material gaps.
compliance_rubric.PARTIALLY_COMPLIANT: Most key concepts are addressed, but several structural indicators are missing, implicit, or incomplete. The requirement is only partially reflected. NOTE: If the unmet indicators are operational and binary in nature (e.g. a specific notification obligation, a defined timeline, a retention period, a named external authority), their absence cannot be compensated by thematic or conceptual overlap — default to NOT_COMPLIANT in that case.

criteria.title: Post-market monitoring
criteria.requirement: Describe how the framework defines post-market monitoring to gather information relevant to systemic risk assessment and updating, and how independent external evaluators are intended to be enabled under appropriate safeguards, where applicable.
criteria.assessment_question: What post-market monitoring methods are required for assessing and mitigating systemic risk, and under what conditions and safeguards must independent external evaluators be granted access to model versions, chains-of-thought, and safety-reduced model variants, including protections against legal retaliation and responsible vulnerability disclosure timelines?
expected_evidence: The framework describes that post-market monitoring is conducted to gather information about the model’s capabilities, propensities, affordances, and/or effects.
The framework explains that post-market monitoring informs systemic risk acceptability determinations and decisions on framework or report updates.

The framework indicates that a range of post-market monitoring methods may be used, such as collecting end-user feedback, providing reporting or incident channels, engaging with affected stakeholders, monitoring public sources, or supporting independent research.
The framework describes how post-market monitoring applies when the model is integrated into AI systems provided or deployed by the Signatory.
The framework describes whether and under what conditions independent external evaluators are intended to be provided with access to the model for post-market monitoring purposes.

The framework explains the safeguards associated with external evaluator access, such as selection criteria, information security measures, responsible disclosure procedures, and non-retaliation commitments.

compliance_rubric.COMPLIANT: All indicators in 'Expected Evidence' are clearly and explicitly present in the text. The framework demonstrates complete structural implementation of the requirement with no material gaps.
compliance_rubric.PARTIALLY_COMPLIANT: Most key concepts are addressed, but several structural indicators are missing, implicit, or incomplete. The requirement is only partially reflected. NOTE: If the unmet indicators are operational and binary in nature (e.g. a specific notification obligation, a defined timeline, a retention period, a named external authority), their absence cannot be compensated by thematic or conceptual overlap — default to NOT_COMPLIANT in that case.

commitments.title: Risk Acceptance & Decisions
commitments.description: Define acceptance criteria and make proceeding decisions based on risk acceptability

criteria.title: Acceptance criteria
criteria.requirement: Describe how the framework defines and justifies systemic risk acceptance criteria or tiers for each identified systemic risk, including how acceptability is determined at the individual and overall level and how safety margins are incorporated.
criteria.assessment_question: Does the framework describe and justify how systemic risk acceptance criteria or tiers are defined for each identified systemic risk, how they are used to determine acceptability at the individual and overall level, and how appropriate safety margins are incorporated?
expected_evidence: The framework describes how systemic risk acceptance is determined for each identified systemic risk.
The framework defines systemic risk acceptance tiers or, where tiers are not suitable and the risk is not a specified systemic risk, alternative acceptance criteria.

The framework explains that acceptance tiers or criteria are measurable and are defined primarily in terms of model capabilities, and may also incorporate model propensities, risk estimates, or other suitable metrics.
The framework indicates that at least one systemic risk tier exists that has not yet been reached by the model.
The framework describes how acceptance criteria or tiers are used to determine acceptability for each systemic risk and for the overall systemic risk.

The framework explains how safety margins are incorporated into acceptance determination, taking into account uncertainty, potential future changes in model capabilities or usage, limitations of risk assessments, and the effectiveness of safety and security mitigations.

compliance_rubric.COMPLIANT: All indicators in 'Expected Evidence' are clearly and explicitly present in the text. The framework demonstrates complete structural implementation of the requirement with no material gaps.
compliance_rubric.PARTIALLY_COMPLIANT: Most key concepts are addressed, but several structural indicators are missing, implicit, or incomplete. The requirement is only partially reflected. NOTE: If the unmet indicators are operational and binary in nature (e.g. a specific notification obligation, a defined timeline, a retention period, a named external authority), their absence cannot be compensated by thematic or conceptual overlap — default to NOT_COMPLIANT in that case.

criteria.title: Proceeding decisions
criteria.requirement: Describe how the framework defines decisions to proceed with, restrict, or not proceed with the development, making available on the market, or use of the model based on systemic risk acceptance determination.
criteria.assessment_question: Does the framework describe how decisions to proceed or not proceed with the development, market availability, or use of the model are made based on systemic risk acceptance determination, and how responses are defined when risks are not acceptable or are likely to become unacceptable?
expected_evidence: The framework states that proceeding with development, making available on the market, or use of the model is conditional on systemic risks being determined acceptable.
The framework states that if systemic risks are not acceptable or are reasonably foreseeable to soon become unacceptable, appropriate measures must be taken before proceeding.
The framework describes possible actions when risks are not acceptable, such as not making the model available on the market, restricting availability, withdrawing, or recalling the model.
The framework states that safety and/or security mitigations are to be implemented when systemic risks are not acceptable.

The framework states that another round of systemic risk identification, systemic risk analysis, and systemic risk acceptance determination is to be conducted when risks are not acceptable or are reasonably foreseeable to soon become unacceptable.

compliance_rubric.COMPLIANT: All indicators in 'Expected Evidence' are clearly and explicitly present in the text. The framework demonstrates complete structural implementation of the requirement with no material gaps.
compliance_rubric.PARTIALLY_COMPLIANT: Most key concepts are addressed, but several structural indicators are missing, implicit, or incomplete. The requirement is only partially reflected. NOTE: If the unmet indicators are operational and binary in nature (e.g. a specific notification obligation, a defined timeline, a retention period, a named external authority), their absence cannot be compensated by thematic or conceptual overlap — default to NOT_COMPLIANT in that case.

commitments.title: Safety Mitigations
commitments.description: Implement safety mitigations robust under adversarial pressure

criteria.title: Safety mitigations
criteria.requirement: The framework defines and requires the implementation of safety mitigations along the entire model lifecycle that are appropriate to identified systemic risks, sufficiently robust under adversarial pressure, and taking into account the model’s release and distribution strategy, with the objective of ensuring that systemic risks are acceptable
criteria.assessment_question: Does the framework define and require the implementation of safety mitigations along the entire model lifecycle that are appropriate to identified systemic risks, sufficiently robust under adversarial pressure, and taking into account the model’s release and distribution strategy, with the objective of ensuring that systemic risks are acceptable?
expected_evidence: The framework describes categories or types of safety mitigations intended to address systemic risks.
The framework explains that safety mitigations are designed to be robust under adversarial pressure, such as attempts to bypass or subvert them.
The framework indicates that safety mitigations take into account the model's release and distribution strategy.
The framework provides examples of safety mitigation approaches, such as e.g. input or output filtering, behaviour modification, staged access, or other emerging safety techniques.
The framework indicates that safety mitigations are considered and adapted across the model lifecycle.

compliance_rubric.COMPLIANT: All indicators in 'Expected Evidence' are clearly and explicitly present in the text. The framework demonstrates complete structural implementation of the requirement with no material gaps.
compliance_rubric.PARTIALLY_COMPLIANT: Most key concepts are addressed, but several structural indicators are missing, implicit, or incomplete. The requirement is only partially reflected. NOTE: If the unmet indicators are operational and binary in nature (e.g. a specific notification obligation, a defined timeline, a retention period, a named external authority), their absence cannot be compensated by thematic or conceptual overlap — default to NOT_COMPLIANT in that case.

commitments.title: Security Protections
commitments.description: Define security goals and implement appropriate security mitigations

criteria.title: Security goal
criteria.requirement: Describe a Security Goal that defines which threat actors the model’s security mitigations are intended to protect against, taking into account the current and expected capabilities of the model.
criteria.assessment_question: Does the framework explicitly define a Security Goal that identifies relevant threat actors (including non-state external and insider threats) and explains how this goal takes into account the model’s current and expected capabilities?
expected_evidence: The framework explicitly defines a Security Goal describing the purpose and scope of security protections for the model.
The Security Goal identifies relevant threat actors, including non-state external threats, insider threats, and other expected threat actors.
The framework explains how the Security Goal takes into account the current capabilities of the model.
The framework indicates that expected or foreseeable future model capabilities are considered when defining the Security Goal.

compliance_rubric.COMPLIANT: All indicators in 'Expected Evidence' are clearly and explicitly present in the text. The framework demonstrates complete structural implementation of the requirement with no material gaps.
compliance_rubric.PARTIALLY_COMPLIANT: Most key concepts are addressed, but several structural indicators are missing, implicit, or incomplete. The requirement is only partially reflected. NOTE: If the unmet indicators are operational and binary in nature (e.g. a specific notification obligation, a defined timeline, a retention period, a named external authority), their absence cannot be compensated by thematic or conceptual overlap — default to NOT_COMPLIANT in that case.

criteria.title: Security mitigations
criteria.requirement: The framework defines how security mitigations are selected and applied to meet the Security Goal, including alignment with the mitigation objectives set out in Appendix 4 or the use of equivalent alternative measures, and specifies that implementation may be staged across the model lifecycle as model capabilities increase.
criteria.assessment_question: Does the framework describe how security mitigations are selected to meet the Security Goal, how the mitigation objectives defined in Appendix 4 (e.g. general security, protection of unreleased model parameters, interface hardening, insider threat protection, and security assurance) are addressed or achieved through equivalent measures, and how implementation is intended to be staged over the model lifecycle?
expected_evidence: The framework explicitly links security mitigations to achieving a defined Security Goal.

The framework indicates that security mitigations align with the mitigation objectives defined in Appendix 4, such as general security protections, protection of unreleased model parameters, interface-access hardening, insider threat mitigation, and security assurance.

The framework indicates that specific security measures may vary depending on organisational context or infrastructure, and explains how alternative mitigations achieve equivalent mitigation objectives.
The framework indicates that security mitigations may be implemented in stages as model capabilities increase along the model lifecycle.

commitments.title: Model Reporting
commitments.description: Create and maintain comprehensive Model Reports with required documentation

criteria.title: Model description
criteria.requirement: Describe, in the Model Report, the model’s characteristics and intended behaviour, including a high-level description of the model, its intended and actual uses, version differences, and a model specification explaining how the model is intended to operate.
criteria.assessment_question: Does the Model Report describe the model’s architecture, capabilities, propensities, and affordances, its intended and actual uses, differences between model versions, and its intended behaviour (including principles, refusal topics, and system prompt or references thereto)?
expected_evidence: The Model Report provides a high-level description of the model’s architecture, capabilities, propensities, and affordances, and how it differs from other models made available by the provider.
The Model Report describes how the model has been used and how it is intended to be used, including relevant use contexts.
The Model Report identifies model versions that are or will be made available on the market and explains relevant differences, including differences in systemic risks or mitigations.

The Model Report includes or references a model specification describing how the model is intended to operate, including guiding principles, prioritisation of instructions, refusal topics, and the system prompt (or a valid reference to it).

compliance_rubric.COMPLIANT: All indicators in 'Expected Evidence' are clearly and explicitly present in the text. The framework demonstrates complete structural implementation of the requirement with no material gaps.
compliance_rubric.PARTIALLY_COMPLIANT: Most key concepts are addressed, but several structural indicators are missing, implicit, or incomplete. The requirement is only partially reflected. criteria.title: Reasons for proceeding
criteria.requirement: Describe how the Model Report explains the reasons for proceeding with the development, making available on the market, and/or use of the model, based on the systemic risk acceptance determination defined in the framework (including safety margins).
criteria.assessment_question: Does the Model Report explain the reasons for proceeding in a way that is consistent with the framework’s systemic risk acceptance determination, including safety margins, foreseeable undermining conditions, and the described decision-making process?
expected_evidence: The Model Report explains why the systemic risks are considered acceptable for this model, with reference to the acceptance logic and safety margins defined in the framework.
The Model Report identifies foreseeable conditions under which this justification would no longer hold.
The Model Report describes how the decision to proceed was made in line with the framework, including whether external actors or independent external evaluators informed the decision.

compliance_rubric.COMPLIANT: All indicators in 'Expected Evidence' are clearly and explicitly present in the text. The framework demonstrates complete structural implementation of the requirement with no material gaps.

criteria.title: Risk documentation
criteria.requirement: Describe in the Model Report how the systemic risk framework has been applied to the model, including reported results of systemic risk identification, analysis, modelling, estimation, evaluations, implemented safety and security mitigations, their limitations, and planned future developments.
criteria.assessment_question: What must the Model Report document regarding systemic risk identification, modelling, and estimates, including model evaluation results with random input/output samples, comparisons between safety-mitigated and fully elicited model versions, independent external evaluator access, Security Goal and mitigation limitations, and planned future model capabilities over the next six months?
expected_evidence: The Model Report describes the reported results of systemic risk identification and analysis, including assumptions, uncertainties, and links to the risk types defined in the framework.
The Model Report explains the reported outcomes of systemic risk modelling and estimation, including how risks are characterised and compared with and without mitigations.
The Model Report summarises relevant model evaluation approaches and results used to understand systemic risks, including illustrative examples or references.
The Model Report describes implemented safety mitigations and their limitations, and explains how they align with the framework’s safety requirements.
The Model Report describes the Security Goal and implemented security mitigations, including alignment with defined mitigation objectives or justified alternatives.
The Model Report includes a high-level forward-looking description of planned model developments and anticipated changes to capabilities, risks, and mitigations.

compliance_rubric.COMPLIANT: All indicators in 'Expected Evidence' are clearly and explicitly present in the text. The framework demonstrates complete structural implementation of the requirement with no material gaps.
compliance_rubric.PARTIALLY_COMPLIANT: Most key concepts are addressed, but several structural indicators are missing, implicit, or incomplete. The requirement is only partially reflected. criteria.title: External reports
criteria.requirement: Describe how the Model Report provides or references any available external reports from independent external model evaluators (Appendix 3.5) and independent external security reviews (Appendix 4.5), subject to confidentiality and evaluator control of publication, and how the report addresses cases where no external evaluator was involved or where evaluator selection must be justified.
criteria.assessment_question: Does the Model Report (i) include or link to any available reports from independent external evaluators and independent external security reviews in a manner consistent with confidentiality and evaluator control, (ii) justify the absence of an external evaluator where applicable, and (iii) explain evaluator selection based on qualification criteria when an evaluator was involved?
expected_evidence: The Model Report includes or references (e.g., via valid hyperlinks) any available reports from independent external evaluators involved under Appendix 3.5, and any available independent external security review reports under Appendix 4.5, where sharing is compatible with confidentiality obligations and external-party control over publication.
The Model Report indicates that inclusion of external reports does not imply endorsement of their content by the Signatory.

If no independent external evaluator was involved, the Model Report provides a justification referencing the applicable exception conditions (e.g., similarly safe or safer model, or inability to appoint qualified evaluators despite early search efforts).

If at least one independent external evaluator was involved, the Model Report explains the choice of evaluator with reference to qualification criteria (e.g., domain expertise, technical evaluation experience, appropriate security protocols, confidentiality protections).

compliance_rubric.COMPLIANT: All indicators in 'Expected Evidence' are clearly and explicitly present in the text. The framework demonstrates complete structural implementation of the requirement with no material gaps.
compliance_rubric.PARTIALLY_COMPLIANT: Most key concepts are addressed, but several structural indicators are missing, implicit, or incomplete. The requirement is only partially reflected. criteria.title: Risk landscape changes
criteria.requirement: Describe, in the Model Report, information relevant for understanding whether and how the development, making available on the market, and/or use of the model results in material changes to the systemic risk landscape that are relevant for systemic risk assessment and mitigation under this Chapter.
criteria.assessment_question: Does the Model Report describe whether and how the model results in material changes to the systemic risk landscape, in a way that supports understanding of implications for systemic risk assessment and mitigation?
expected_evidence: The Model Report includes information enabling understanding of whether and how the model leads to material changes in the systemic risk landscape relevant for systemic risk assessment and mitigation.
The Model Report describes scaling effects or scaling laws that suggest novel or materially enhanced model capabilities, where applicable.
The Model Report summarises characteristics of novel model architectures that materially improve computational efficiency or capabilities, where applicable.
The Model Report describes information relevant to assessing changes in mitigation effectiveness (e.g., reduced human legibility of chain-of-thought or other safety-relevant properties).
The Model Report describes training techniques that materially improve the efficiency or feasibility of training (e.g., distributed or large-scale training), where applicable.

criteria.title: Model report updates
criteria.requirement: Describe whether and how the framework defines obligations to update the Model Report when the justification for systemic risk acceptability is materially undermined, and whether it specifies periodic update expectations, versioning, and changelog requirements in line with Measure 7.6.
criteria.assessment_question: Does the framework acknowledge and describe when Model Report updates are required, including material undermining triggers, timing expectations, periodic updates for most capable models, and versioning/changelog requirements?
expected_evidence: The framework describes circumstances under which the justification for systemic risk acceptability may be materially undermined and requires a Model Report update in such cases.
The framework lists or references example triggers for updates, such as material changes in model capabilities, use, integrations, serious incidents, or evaluation validity.
The framework specifies expectations regarding the timing of Model Report updates, including completion within a reasonable time and prior to market release for deliberate changes.
The framework acknowledges periodic Model Report updates for the most capable models (e.g. at least every six months), including applicable exemptions.
The framework requires updated Model Reports to include versioning information and a changelog explaining how and why updates were made.

criteria.title: AI Office notification
criteria.requirement: Describe whether and how the framework acknowledges the obligation to provide the AI Office with access to the Model Report and any updates, including timing requirements, permitted delays, interim reporting, and conditions for redactions in line with Measure 7.7.
criteria.assessment_question: Does the framework acknowledge and describe obligations to provide the AI Office with access to the Model Report and updates, including timing rules, permitted delays with interim reporting, and conditions for redactions?
expected_evidence: The framework states that the AI Office must be provided with access to the Model Report by the time a model is placed on the market.
The framework specifies that updates to the Model Report must be made accessible to the AI Office within a defined timeframe (e.g. five business days).
The framework acknowledges conditions under which redactions may be applied, such as requirements arising from national security laws.
The framework describes the possibility of delaying provision of the Model Report or updates for a limited period (e.g. up to 15 business days), subject to good-faith conditions.
The framework requires or acknowledges the provision of an interim Model Report containing specified information when such a delay is used.
The framework describes acceptable access mechanisms (e.g. publicly accessible link or secure channel specified by the AI Office).

commitments.title: Governance & Resources
commitments.description: Establish governance, allocate resources, and promote healthy risk culture

criteria.title: Responsibilities
criteria.requirement: Describe whether and how the framework defines clear responsibilities for managing systemic risks, including systemic risk oversight, ownership, support and monitoring, and assurance, and how these responsibilities are allocated across organisational governance levels.
criteria.assessment_question: How must signatories assign systemic risk oversight, ownership, support and monitoring, and assurance responsibilities across the management body in its supervisory and executive functions, operational teams, and internal and external assurance providers, including role-specific requirements such as separation from core business activities and cascading responsibility structures for SMEs and SMCs?
expected_evidence: The framework explicitly defines responsibilities for systemic risk oversight, ownership, support and monitoring, and assurance.
The framework describes how systemic risk oversight is assigned to a supervisory or otherwise independent governance function.
The framework describes how systemic risk ownership is assigned to executive-level functions responsible for risk-producing activities.
The framework describes how systemic risk support and monitoring is assigned to functions that are organisationally independent from core business activities.
The framework describes how systemic risk assurance is provided to a supervisory or independent body, including internal and, where applicable, external assurance.
The framework acknowledges proportional allocation of responsibilities based on organisational size and complexity (e.g. SMEs or SMCs).

criteria.title: Resources
criteria.requirement: Describe whether and how the framework specifies that management bodies oversee the allocation of resources appropriate to the systemic risks, for actors assigned systemic risk responsibilities.
criteria.assessment_question: Does the framework explicitly acknowledge management oversight of resource allocation for systemic risk responsibilities and specify the categories of resources to be considered?
expected_evidence: The framework explicitly states that management bodies oversee the allocation of resources for managing systemic risks.
The framework links resource allocation to the responsibilities defined for systemic risk management.
The framework specifies that resource allocation must be appropriate to the systemic risks stemming from the model.
The framework explicitly identifies human resources as a required resource category for systemic risk management.
The framework explicitly identifies financial resources as a required resource category for systemic risk management.
The framework explicitly identifies access to information and knowledge as a required resource category for systemic risk management.
The framework explicitly identifies computational resources as a required resource category for systemic risk management.

criteria.requirement: Describe whether and how the framework promotes a healthy systemic risk culture, including leadership tone, openness to challenge, independence of systemic risk functions, and protections for reporting and whistleblowing.
criteria.assessment_question: Does the framework explicitly acknowledge the importance of a healthy systemic risk culture and describe principles or mechanisms intended to promote it?

The framework states that challenge, escalation, or critical discussion of systemic risk decisions is encouraged.

commitments.title: Serious Incident Management
commitments.description: Identify, document, report, and retain serious incident information

criteria.title: Incident identification
criteria.requirement: Describe whether and how the framework defines methods and channels for identifying and tracking serious incidents across the model lifecycle, including consideration of external sources and facilitation of third-party reporting.
criteria.assessment_question: Does the framework acknowledge and describe approaches for identifying serious incidents, including monitoring external sources and enabling third-party reporting?
expected_evidence: The framework explicitly recognises the need to identify and track serious incidents across the model lifecycle.
The framework describes intended methods for identifying serious incidents, including reference to post-market monitoring or similar processes.
The framework references consideration of external information sources (e.g. media, incident databases, research, public reporting).
The framework describes how downstream providers, users, or other third parties may report serious incidents to the Signatory and/or authorities.

criteria.title: Incident documentation
criteria.requirement: Describe whether and how the framework defines the information that should be tracked, documented, and reported for serious incidents, including root cause analysis, mitigation failures or circumvention, and handling of incomplete information.
criteria.assessment_question: Does the framework specify the minimum information set for serious incident documentation and reporting, including root cause analysis and treatment of missing information?
expected_evidence: The framework defines the categories of information to be documented for serious incidents (e.g. timing, harm, affected groups, model involvement, and response actions).
The framework specifies that a root cause analysis should be conducted, including examination of model outputs, inputs, and any failures or circumvention of systemic risk mitigations.
The framework describes how patterns or signals from post-market monitoring may inform incident analysis.
The framework acknowledges that some information may be unavailable at the time of reporting and requires such gaps to be explicitly recorded.
The framework indicates that the level of detail in serious incident documentation should be proportionate to the severity of the incident.

criteria.title: Reporting timelines
criteria.requirement: The framework defines mandatory severity-based reporting timelines for serious incidents involving the model, including differentiated deadlines for initial reports based on incident type, requirements for periodic intermediate updates for unresolved incidents, and submission of a final report after incident resolution.
criteria.assessment_question: Does the framework specify clearly differentiated reporting timelines for serious incidents (including specific deadlines for initial notification based on severity), periodic intermediate updates for unresolved incidents, and a final report after resolution?
expected_evidence: The framework defines categories of serious incidents (e.g., critical infrastructure disruption, cybersecurity breach, death, serious harm to health, fundamental rights, property or environment).
The framework associates specific deadlines for initial reporting following awareness of a serious incident (e.g., two days, five days, ten days, fifteen days depending on severity).
The framework clarifies that reporting is triggered when the organisation becomes aware of the involvement of its model, including cases of reasonable suspicion of causal linkage.
The framework specifies periodic intermediate updates for unresolved serious incidents (e.g., at least every four weeks after the initial report).
The framework defines a deadline for submitting a final report after resolution of the serious incident (e.g., within 60 days after resolution).
The framework allows aggregation of multiple similar serious incidents within defined reporting timelines while respecting the earliest applicable deadline.

criteria.title: Retention
criteria.requirement: Define and maintain retention requirements ensuring that serious-incident documentation is preserved for at least five years from the later of the documentation date or the incident date.
criteria.assessment_question: Does the framework define retention requirements for serious-incident documentation that meet or exceed a five-year period?
expected_evidence: The framework states that documentation of information related to serious incidents must be retained.
The framework specifies a minimum retention period of at least five years.
The framework indicates that the retention period is counted from the later of the documentation date or the date of the serious incident.

NOTE: If the unmet indicators are operational and binary in nature (e.g. a specific notification obligation, a defined timeline, a retention period, a named external authority), their absence cannot be compensated by thematic or conceptual overlap — default to NOT_COMPLIANT in that case.
compliance_rubric.NOT_COMPLIANT: The retrieved text does not meaningfully address the core intent, OR the text addresses related governance themes but the specific operational mechanism required by this criterion (e.g. a regulatory obligation, a defined procedure, a named authority) is entirely absent.
compliance_rubric.NOT_EVIDENCED / UNKNOWN: Retrieved documentation is insufficient to determine whether the specific indicators and structural requirements are addressed.
